coredata.dc:title: Low-temperature rapid synthesis of nitrogen and phosphorus dual-doped carbon dots for multicolor cellular imaging and hemoglobin probing in human blood
coredata.dc:creator: Huang, Shan
Yang, Erli
Liu, Yi
Yao, Jiandong
Su, Wei
Xiao, Qi
coredata.dc:description: Heteroatom doping of carbon dots (CDs) can efficiently tune the structure of electronic energy levels and modulate the photochemical properties of CDs. However, the reported synthesis routes of heteroatom-doped CDs are complicated and rigorous. Herein, a simple and rapid low-temperature heating strategy was applied to synthesize fluorescent nitrogen and phosphorus dual-doped CDs (N,P-CDs) using sucrose as carbon source, and 1,2-ethylenediamine (EDA) and phosphoric acid as dopants, respectively. These N,P-CDs exhibit excellent fluorescent stability over wide pH range solutions (4–11), ultrahigh ion strength (3 M KCl), and longtime UV light irradiation (3 h continuously), which makes these N,P-QDs promising good candidates for fluorescent probes. These N,P-CDs showed excellent biocompatibility, multicolor cellular imaging, and non-cytotoxicity to human hepatocellular carcinoma HepG2 cells even at 500 μg/mL levels for 48 h incubation. These N,P-CDs exhibited a strong blue emission and a sensitive response to hemoglobin. The N,P-CDs based fluorescent probe was then applied to sensitively determine hemoglobin with a detection limit of 0.29 nM. Notably, it was also applied for label-free detection of hemoglobin in human urine samples and human blood samples, which indicated its potential applications in clinical diagnosis and other biologically related study.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Nitrogen
Phosphorus
Dual-doped carbon dots
Multicolor cellular imaging
Hemoglobin probing
Human blood

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Confocal microscopy imaging of living cells
Cytotoxicity investigation and multicolor cellular imaging of N,P-CDs

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was financially supported by National Natural Science Foundation of China ( 21763005 , 21563006 , 21403039 ), Natural Science Foundation of Guangxi Province ( 2015GXNSFAA139033 , 2016GXNSFBA380118 , 2017GXNSFDA198034 , 2017GXNSFFA198005 ), Guangxi Scientific and Technological Development Projects ( AD17195081 ), the high-level-innovation team (guijiaoren[2017]38) and outstanding scholar project of Guangxi Higher Education Institutes , BAGUI Scholar Program of Guangxi Province of China , and Project of Outstanding Young Teachers’ Training in Higher Education Institutions of Guangxi . Appendix A

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Typical TEM (a) and HRTEM images (b) of N,P-CDs. Insert: diameter size distribution histogram (a) and magnification of a single nanoparticle (b).
FT-IR spectrum (a), XPS survey spectrum (b), C 1 s XPS spectrum (c), N 1 s XPS spectrum (d), O 1 s XPS spectrum (e), and P 2p XPS spectrum (f) of N,P-CDs.

(a) Normalized UV–vis absorption spectrum of N,P-CDs, excitation spectrum of N,P-CDs at λ em = 475 nm and emission spectrum of N,P-CDs at λ ex = 380 nm. Insert: Photograph of N,P-CDs under the radiation of white light (Left) and 365 nm UV lamp (Right). (b) Fluorescence spectra of N,P-CDs at different excitation wavelength. Insert: Normalized fluorescence spectra of N,P-CDs at different excitation wavelength.
Human HepG2 cell viability after incubation with different concentrations of N,P-CDs for 24 h (a) and 48 h (b).

Confocal fluorescence images of human HepG2 cells incubated with 500 μg/mL N,P-CDs at 37 °C for 4 h, with excitation wavelength of 402 nm (a), 488 nm (b), 543 nm (c), 638 nm (d), bright field (e), and overlap field (f), respectively.

Fluorescence intensity of N,P-CDs based probes in the absence (black column) and presence (red column) of different biomolecules (the concentration of each biomolecules was 5 μM) (a, b) and various chemical substances (the concentration of each chemical substances was 1 mM) (c). (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

(a) Fluorescence decay traces of N,P-CDs and N,P-CDs-Hb system. All measurements were made at λ em = 475 nm. τ is the fluorescence decay time of N,P-CDs, and b is the normalized preexponential factor, respectively. (b) CD spectra of N,P-CDs, Hb, and N,P-CDs-Hb system.

(a) Fluorescence spectra of N,P-CDs in different concentrations of Hb (from top to bottom): 0, 1, 5, 10, 30, 60, 100, 300, 500, 700, 1200, 2000, 3000, 4000, 6000, 7000, 8000, and 10000 nM. Insert: Photograph of N,P-CDs under the radiation of 365 nm UV lamp before (Left) and after (Right) the addition of Hb. (b) Relationship between the change of fluorescence intensity of N,P-CDs and the concentration of Hb from 0 to 10 μM. Insert: Linear relationship between the change of fluorescence intensity of N,P-CDs and the concentration of Hb from 1 to 6000 nM.

head.ce:title: Low-temperature rapid synthesis of nitrogen and phosphorus dual-doped carbon dots for multicolor cellular imaging and hemoglobin probing in human blood

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Chemistry and Materials Science, Guangxi Key Laboratory of Natural Polymer Chemistry and Physics, Guangxi Teachers Education University, Nanning 530001, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Chemistry and Materials Science
Guangxi Key Laboratory of Natural Polymer Chemistry and Physics

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Chemistry and Molecular Sciences, Wuhan University, Wuhan 430072, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Chemistry and Molecular Sciences

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: College of Chemistry and Materials Science, Guangxi Key Laboratory of Natural Polymer Chemistry and Physics, Guangxi Teachers Education University, Nanning 530001, PR China.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Chemistry and Materials Science, Guangxi Key Laboratory of Natural Polymer Chemistry and Physics, Guangxi Teachers Education University, Nanning 530001, PR China.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Heteroatom doping of carbon dots (CDs) can efficiently tune the structure of electronic energy levels and modulate the photochemical properties of CDs. However, the reported synthesis routes of heteroatom-doped CDs are complicated and rigorous. Herein, a simple and rapid low-temperature heating strategy was applied to synthesize fluorescent nitrogen and phosphorus dual-doped CDs (N,P-CDs) using sucrose as carbon source, and 1,2-ethylenediamine (EDA) and phosphoric acid as dopants, respectively. These N,P-CDs exhibit excellent fluorescent stability over wide pH range solutions (4–11), ultrahigh ion strength (3 M KCl), and longtime UV light irradiation (3 h continuously), which makes these N,P-QDs promising good candidates for fluorescent probes. These N,P-CDs showed excellent biocompatibility, multicolor cellular imaging, and non-cytotoxicity to human hepatocellular carcinoma HepG2 cells even at 500 μg/mL levels for 48 h incubation. These N,P-CDs exhibited a strong blue emission and a sensitive response to hemoglobin. The N,P-CDs based fluorescent probe was then applied to sensitively determine hemoglobin with a detection limit of 0.29 nM. Notably, it was also applied for label-free detection of hemoglobin in human urine samples and human blood samples, which indicated its potential applications in clinical diagnosis and other biologically related study.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon dots (CDs) are a fascinating class of “zero-dimensional” carbon nanomaterials that comprise of discrete, quasi-spherical nanoparticles with a diameter less than 10 nm [1–3]. As a rising star of carbon-based fluorescent nanomaterials, CDs have attracted tremendous attentions for a wide variety of diverse promising bioapplications [4–7], because of their high chemical inertness, low toxicity, excellent biocompatibility, and cell membrane permeability [8]. Heteroatom doping of CDs is an emerging field of carbon-based nanomaterials science [9,10]. The facile access of CDs makes heteroatom doping available, and such doping can tune the structure of electronic energy levels and local chemical properties of CDs efficiently [11]. Up to now, masses of efforts have been made to prepare nitrogen (N)-doped, sulfur (S)-doped, phosphorus (P)-doped, N,S-doped, or N,P-doped CDs from natural biomass, amino acid, peptide, and other small molecule [9–14]. It is well-known that N-doped CDs can not only improve fluorescence properties of CDs but also allow to provide available functional groups for target sensing [15]. Other larger atoms (S or P) have the most preferable influences on the polarizability of carbon-based nanomaterials and thus on their performance in biological applications [11,12]. Therefore, the electronic characteristics of CDs can be altered by doping with nitrogen and phosphorus atoms by creating more active sites and unanticipated properties [14]. Although many approaches are reported for preparing nitrogen and phosphorus dual-doped CDs (N,P-CDs), some synthetic pathways suffer from the application of harsh synthesis conditions and tedious processes, such as rigorous synthesis temperature (all higher than 130 °C) [14,16–18] and complicated heating process (heating rate 1.5 °C/min to 180 °C) [16]. Therefore, it is still a great challenge and inevitable to achieve simple, rapid, and cost-efficient synthesis routes in one strategy.

Hemoglobin (Hb), which is the main tetrameric metalloprotein composed of a globular protein part and four iron-containing heme parts, plays important role in transporting molecular oxygen in mammalian blood [19]. The amount of Hb is associated with several clinical disease, so the easy and accurate quantitative analysis of Hb in clinical diagnostics is essential to assess the extent of diseases [20]. In the past few years, the design and development of fluorescent CDs probes for Hb is becoming an important project because of their simplicity, easy monitoring, rapid response, and high sensitivity. Barati et al. achieved highly selective detection of Hb (with a detection limit of 0.4 nM) by CDs synthesized through one-step hydrothermal method [21]. Our group proposed a Hb assay with a 0.12 nM detection limit using CDs-based fluorescent probe, which was then applied for the fluorescence detection of Hb in human urine samples and human blood samples [22]. Indeed, most of the fluorescent probes for Hb are signaled by fluorescence quenching originating from the special binding interaction between Hb and active sites on the surface of CDs. Therefore, preparing sites rich CDs that are prone to binding with Hb is still imminently desired in the detection of trace Hb in organisms.

Herein, a simple and rapid low-temperature heating approach has been designed to obtain the fluorescent N,P-CDs that use sucrose as carbon source, and 1,2-ethylenediamine (EDA) and phosphoric acid as dopants, respectively (Scheme 1 ). The synthesis temperature is reduced as close to the room temperature as possible, which makes this method more simple and convenient. The morphology, surface functional groups, chemical state, and fluorescent properties of N,P-CDs were investigated extensively. Due to the doping of nitrogen and phosphorus atoms, N,P-CDs exhibited sufficient water-solubility and excellent fluorescent properties in ultrahigh ion strength, wide pH range solutions, and longtime UV light irradiation. N,P-CDs show negligible cytotoxicity and excellent biocompatibility, which is of crucial importance for many biomedical applications. Moreover, the label-free N,P-CDs-based fluorescent probe showed a sensitive response to Hb in ultra-wide concentration range of 1–6000 nM with the detection limit of 0.29 nM. This proposed fluorescent probe based on N,P-CDs was further applied for the detection of Hb in real human samples, which broadened the biological applications of carbon-based nanomaterials significantly.

Sucrose, EDA, phosphoric acid, and quinine sulfate were purchased from Aladdin Chemistry Co., Ltd (Shanghai, China). Hb, amino acids, and proteins were obtained from Sigma Reagents Company (St. Louis, MO, USA). Human hepatocellular carcinoma HepG2 cells were obtained from Guangxi Medical University, China. All biological reagents were purchased from Thermo Fisher (Suzhou) Instrument Co., Ltd (Suzhou, China). All other chemicals were obtained from Shanghai Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd (Shanghai, China). All chemicals were of the highest commercially available purity and were used as received without further purification. Ultrapure water with resistivity of 18.2 MΩ cm was prepared from a Millipore-Q Academic purification system (Bedford, MA) and was used in all experiments.

A low-temperature heating approach was used to prepare N,P-CDs (Scheme 1). Firstly, sucrose (1 g) and phosphoric acid (2.0 mL) with 2.0 mL ultrapure water were mixed uniformly. The mixture was heated at 80 °C for 50 min, then the heat was removed and the solution was cooled to room temperature gradually. The resulting dark brown mixture was placed into the ice-water bath. Then, EDA (6.0 mL) was added into the mixture under vigorous stirring, and the brown mushy N,P-CDs solution was obtained after the acid-base neutralization process between EDA and phosphoric acid. Subsequently, 40 mL ultrapure water was added to dilute the solution. The crude product was filtered through a 0.22 μm cellulose filter paper and then subjected to dialysis for 72 h in a dialysis bag with a cut-off molecular weight of 500 Da (Shanghai Green Bird Science & Technology Development Co., China). The khaki solid N,P-CDs products were obtained under vacuum freeze-drying for 48 h. Finally, the obtained N,P-CDs were stored at 4 °C for later applications.

UV–vis absorption spectra were recorded on Shimadzu UV-3600 Plus UV-VIS-NIR spectrophotometer (Shimadzu, Japan). All fluorescence spectra were recorded on Thermo Scientific Lumina fluorescence spectrometer (Thermo Fisher Scientific, USA). Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR) spectra were performed on Thermo Nicolet iS10 spectrometer (Thermo Fisher Scientific, USA). Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) and high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM) images were recorded on JEM 2100 LaB6 high-resolution transmission electron microscope (JEM, Japan). The sample for TEM characterization was prepared by placing a drop of N,P-CDs solution on carbon film-coated copper grid and dried at room temperature. X-ray diffraction (XRD) was recorded on Brucker D-8 advance powder X-ray diffractometer (Brucker, Germany). X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was acquired from ESCALAB 250Xi X-ray Photoelectron Spectroscopy (Thermo Fisher Scientific, USA). Elemental analysis was obtained using Elementar Analysensysteme Vario EL/Micro Cube organic element analyzer (Elementar Analysensysteme GmbH, Germany). Confocal fluorescence microscopy images were obtained using Nikon A1 confocal laser scanning microscope (Nikon, Japan). Circular dichroism (CD) spectra were recorded on Chirascan CD spectrometer (Applied Photophysics, England). The pH values of all solution were measured using a Sartorius PB-10 pH meter (Sartorius, China).

Cytotoxicity of N,P-CDs on human HepG2 cells was calculated by MTT (3-(4,5-dimethylthiazol-2-yl)-2,5-diphenyltetra-zolium bromide) assay. Firstly, human HepG2 cells were grown in a humidified atmosphere containing 5% CO2 at 37 °C in Dulbecco’s modified eagle medium (DMEM) medium supplemented with 10% FBS and 1% penicillin-streptomycin solution. Then, human HepG2 cells were seeded in 96-well plates at 2 × 104 cells/well in DMEM medium and incubated at 37 °C in a humidified 5% CO2 incubator for 24 h. The cells were seeded in triplicate with fresh medium including grade concentrations of N,P-CDs (0, 10, 30, 60, 150, 300, and 500 μg/mL) at 37 °C for different times (24 h and 48 h). Then, 20 μL of MTT (5 mg/mL) was added to each well and incubated for additional 4 h. Then, the culture medium was removed and 200 μL of dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO) was added to each well with shaking for 10 min. The optical density (OD) of the purple formazan at 490 nm of each well was collected using an enzymelinked immunosorbent assay (ELISA) plate reader with pure DMSO as a blank. The cytotoxicity was calculated by the following equation: cell viability (%) = ODtreated/ODcontrol × 100, where ODcontrol was obtained in the absence of N,P-CDs, and ODtreated was obtained in the presence of N,P-CDs. Six replicate wells were used for each control and test concentration per well. Results are expressed as their mean standard deviations.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Confocal microscopy imaging of living cells
ce:section.ce:para: Human HepG2 cells were grown in a humidified atmosphere containing 5% CO2 at 37 °C in DMEM medium supplemented with 10% FBS and 1% penicillin-streptomycin solution. Human HepG2 cells were seeded onto 35 mm glass bottom culture dishes for 24 h after the concentration reached 5 × 105 cells/mL. Cells in exponential growth phase were used for experiment (2 × 104 cells/well) and seeded to 96-well plates for overnight. Then, the cells were incubated with 500 μg/mL N,P-CDs for another 4 h and then washed with phosphate buffered solution (pH 7.5) three times. Finally, the confocal microscopy imaging experiments were conducted (20 × objective).

In a typical Hb probing experiment, the concentration of N,P-CDs was kept at 0.5 mg/mL in phosphate buffer (0.1 M, pH 7.5). Then, Hb solution with different concentrations was added into the solution with gentle shaking. After 3 min incubation at room temperature, the fluorescence spectra were measured within the wavelength range of 400–650 nm under 380 nm excitation. The band-slits of excitation and emission were all set as 10.0 nm. The fluorescence intensity of N,P-CDs at 475 nm was used for the quantitative analysis of Hb. The sensitivity and selectivity measurements were conducted in triplicate.

Human urine samples and human blood samples were obtained from healthy male and female adult volunteers in our laboratory. For human urine samples determination, human urine samples were centrifuged at 5000 rpm for 10 min, and then the supernatant was diluted 100-fold with phosphate buffer (0.1 M, pH 7.5) prior to being determined. For human blood samples determination, 1 mL human blood samples were centrifuged at 1500 rpm for 5 min to get rid of serum. The reserved red blood cells were washed for three times with 0.9% physiological saline solution and then dissolved by toluene. After centrifugation at 2000 rpm for 10 min, human blood samples were diluted 1000-fold with phosphate buffer (0.1 M, pH 7.5) before detection.

In the synthesis strategy of N,P-CDs, sucrose is selected as the carbon source, while EDA and phosphoric acid are adopted as the source of nitrogen and phosphorus (Scheme 1). The fluorescent behavior of N,P-CDs is affected by several factors, such as the amount of the carbon source, the volume ratio of the dopants, the reaction temperature, and the reaction time. The preliminary experimental results showed that the optimum synthesis parameters were as follows: 1 g sucrose, volume ratio of phosphoric acid to EDA of 1: 3 (2.0 mL phosphoric acid and 6.0 mL EDA), 80 °C, and 50 min (Fig. S1 to S4, and Table S1). Such synthetic method is much faster and more convenient compared with some reported strategies for N,P-CDs preparation in aqueous solutions which often take several hours and complicated heating process.

The particle size distribution and morphology of N,P-CDs were observed directly via TEM and HRTEM. As displayed in Fig. 1 a, the N,P-CDs possess excellent solution dispersion and are well separated from each other without apparent aggregation. In the selected area, the size distribution of these N,P-CDs is relatively narrow. The majority falls within the range from 1.5 nm to 3.3 nm. This indicates the high level of size selectivity of the synthesis. Based on the statistical analysis of more than one hundred particles dots, almost 70% of N,P-CDs are concentrated at an average size of 2.36 nm (insert in Fig. 1a). The average size of these N,P-CDs is smaller than those of N-CDs (around 4.5 nm) [10] and N,P-CDs (average 3.0 nm and 8.1 nm) [16,18]. The HRTEM image of Fig. 1b shows that the N,P-CDs have nearly spherical shapes. The HRTEM image taken from one nanoparticle (insert in Fig. 1b) displays a highly crystalline structure with lattice spacing of 0.20 nm. This phenomenon may be attributable to the graphitic (sp2) carbon’s (102) diffraction planes of graphitic carbon [18], which is quite similar to the previously reported CDs [23]. Such graphitic carbon in N,P-CDs can be further verified by XRD. It is obvious in Fig. S5 that the XRD pattern of N,P-CDs displays two peaks at 23° and 44°, which are attributed to the graphite lattice spacing (002) and (100) of graphitic carbon [24,25]. Therefore, the lattice spacing of N,P-CDs may be attributed to the graphitic carbon.

The chemical bonds and the structure of N,P-CDs were explored by FT-IR spectrometry. As depicted in Fig. 2 a, the broad absorption peak at 2986 cm−1 and the tiny absorption peak at 1369 cm−1 are attributed to the stretching vibration and the bending vibration of NH bond, respectively [26,27]. The sharp absorption peak at 1076 cm−1 is resulted from the stretching vibration of CN bond [28]. All these absorption peaks indicate the successful doping of nitrogen atoms. The absorption peaks at 3440 cm−1 and 1548 cm−1 are assigned to the stretching vibrations of O−H bond and CC bond, respectively [13,14]. Other characteristic absorption peaks consistent with phosphorus and/or oxygen-containing groups, such as POH bending vibration from 2500 cm−1 to 2750 cm−1 [14], CO stretching vibration of 1643 cm−1 [10], simultaneously, the stretching vibration of PO4 3− resulted in the absorption peak at 547 cm−1 [14,18], are observed, which confirms the presence of phosphorus atoms in the prepared N,P-CDs.

More detail information on the nature and the type of constituted elements present on N,P-CDs were explored by XPS technique. Fig. 2b shows the survey XPS spectrum of N,P-CDs, indicating that N,P-CDs are mainly composed of carbon, nitrogen, oxygen, and phosphorus with five typical characteristic binding energy peaks at 284.96 eV (C 1s), 400.26 eV (N 1s), 531.86 eV (O 1s), 192.78 eV (P 2s), and 132.69 eV (P 2p), respectively. Elemental analysis shows the composition of N,P-CDs as C 21.58 wt%, H 6.79 wt%, N 16.54 wt%, P 15.08 wt%, and O (calculated) 40.01 wt% (Table S2a), indicating that they are nitrogen and phosphorus dual-doped CDs. Moreover, the doping percentages of nitrogen and phosphorus are higher than that of previously reported N,P-CDs [16,18]. Table S2b describes the relative number of atoms which is expressed according to the elemental contents. The empirical formula for N,P-CDs is calculated to be approximately C18H68N12P5O25. The high-resolution C 1s spectrum demonstrates five binding energy peaks at 284.15 eV, 284.86 eV, 285.93 eV, 287.20 eV, and 288.44 eV (Fig. 2c), which are corresponded to the sp2C in CC bond, sp3C in CC bond, sp3C in COH bond, sp2C in CN bond, and sp2C in OCO bond, respectively [29,30]. High-resolution N 1s spectrum also exhibits four binding energy peaks at 399.17 eV, 399.75 eV, 400.46 eV, and 401.25 eV (Fig. 2d) those are associated with CNC, NH, N(C)3, and NN/PN bonds, respectively [29,30]. Two main peaks in high-resolution N 1s spectrum indicate the binding energy at 399.75 eV for N−H bond and at 401.25 eV for NN/PN bond, further confirming that nitrogen atoms are presented in N,P-CDs. High-resolution O 1 s spectrum shows four fitted binding energy peaks at 530.38 eV, 531.39 eV, 532.08 eV, and 533.01 eV (Fig. 2e) those are attributed to OC−O, PO, C−OH, and P−O bonds, respectively [29,30]. High-resolution P 2p spectrum of N,P-CDs can be decomposed into two binding energy peaks at 132.49 eV and 133.42 eV (Fig. 2f), indicating the bonds between PO and PC/PN [29,30]. Therefore, highly agreed with FT-IR results, PO4 3− is indeed covalently bonded to the N,P-CDs. All results of XPS and FT-IR spectra clearly confirm that nitrogen and phosphorus atoms have perfectly doped in the CDs. Due to the introduction of nitrogen and phosphorus atoms, the electronic properties and surface chemical reactivities of N,P-CDs may be improved significantly.

Optical properties of N,P-CDs were investigated from the UV–vis absorption spectrum and fluorescence behavior of N,P-CDs under different excitation wavelengths. As shown in Fig. 3 a, N,P-CDs exhibit two obvious absorption peaks from 200 nm to 420 nm (labeled by star). The former typical absorption peak around 280 nm is suggested to result from the π → π* electronic transition of CC, CN, or N = P groups [16]. The latter intense absorption peak at 330 nm is originated from the n → π* electronic transitions of the CO bond, which is probably due to the trapping of excited-state energy by the defect surface states resulted in strong emission [10,31]. Moreover, the light yellow N,P-CDs aqueous solution emits a strong blue fluorescence under a hand-held UV lamp with 365 nm excitation (insert in Fig. 3a). In addition, as in the case of most of the carbon-based nanomaterials, the emission wavelength of N,P-CDs is red-shifted with the excitation wavelength increasing from 360 to 450 nm (insert in Fig. 3b). As further shown in Fig. 3b, the maximum excitation/emission wavelengths of N,P-CDs are 380/475 nm/nm. Such excitation wavelength-dependent band-edge emission character of N,P-CDs is mainly derived from their different sizes and surface states which affect the band gad of N,P-CDs. The surface state is similar to a molecular state, but the size impact is a result of quantum dimensions, both of which are attributed to the complexity of the excited states of N,P-CDs.

Moreover, the average fluorescence lifetime of N,P-CDs was calculated to be 6.41 ns that was almost the maximum value among the reported doping CDs to our knowledge (Fig. S6) [18,31]. The relative quantum yield (QY) of N,P-CDs was measured to be 12.7% (Fig. S7) using quinine sulfate (QY = 54% under 380 nm excitation) as the reference, which was higher than the reported N,P-CDs [14]. Therefore, these N,P-CDs can be used to construct highly sensitive chemosensor. To investigate the optical stability of N,P-CDs, the influences of pH, ionic strength, and UV light irradiation on their fluorescence intensities were recorded. As presented in Fig. S8, these N,P-CDs are quite stable in wide range of pH values from 4 to 11. Fig. S9 shows the influence of ionic strength on the fluorescence intensity of N,P-CDs in phosphate buffer (0.1 M, pH 7.5) by varying the concentration of NaCl. It is obvious that the fluorescence intensity of N,P-CDs changes slightly even in concentration of NaCl up to 3.0 M, which is resulted from no ionization of the groups located on N,P-CDs surface. Furthermore, the fluorescence intensity of N,P-CDs changes quite tiny even under continuous UV light (365 nm) irradiation for 3 h (Fig. S10), which suggests that N,P-CDs possess excellent fluorescent stability. Moreover, dry N,P-CDs powder can be re-dispersed repeatedly in solution without any aggregation. Even after three months storage, both absorption and fluorescence spectra of these N,P-CDs are still remained constant. According to the above discussions, N,P-CDs have relatively long fluorescence lifetime, high QY, and excellent fluorescent stability under the entire physiological pH range, ultrahigh ionic strength, and long time UV light irradiation, facilitating wider applications of N,P-CDs in probing, labeling, and imaging.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Cytotoxicity investigation and multicolor cellular imaging of N,P-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: In order to demonstrate the future biomedical applications of N,P-CDs, the biocompatible properties of N,P-CDs were investigated by incubating different concentrations of N,P-CDs with human HepG2 cells for different times. As illustrated in Fig. 4 a, N,P-CDs are nearly non-cytotoxic to human HepG2 cells even in concentration of up to 500 μg/mL for 24 h. When the incubation time is prolonged to 48 h, the human HepG2 cells viability remains almost 95% of the initial value (Fig. 4b). These results indicate that N,P-CDs can not affect the viability of human HepG2 cells significantly. It is worthy to note that this concentration of N,P-CDs is much higher (500 μg/mL) and the incubation time is much longer (48 h) than those required for the potential bioimaging applications. These results confirm the low cytotoxicity and excellent biomedical compatibility of these N,P-CDs which can be suitable for future biomedical applications.

These N,P-CDs were used for multicolor cellular imaging in human HepG2 cells to expand and validate their bioimaging applications. When human HepG2 cells are incubated with 500 μg/mL N,P-CDs for 2 h, the shape of human HepG2 cells do not change and the cell structure integrity of human HepG2 cells is maintained (Fig. S11). This suggests that the normal morphology of human HepG2 cells is not affected obviously by N,P-CDs. As is shown in Fig. 5 , when human HepG2 cells are incubated with 500 μg/mL N,P-CDs solution at 37 °C for 4 h, N,P-CDs can penetrate human HepG2 cells and exhibit strong fluorescence inside the cells. These N,P-CDs-stained human HepG2 cells display blue (Fig. 5a), green (Fig. 5b), red (Fig. 5c), and dark red (Fig. 5d) emissions when they are excited at 402 nm, 488 nm, 543 nm, and 638 nm lasers, respectively, further demonstrating the potential applications of these N,P-CDs as multicolor bioimaging agents for living cells. The bright-field image of these N,P-CDs-stained human HepG2 (Fig. 5e) shows clearly the normal morphology of human HepG2 cells, which verifies that N,P-CDs are biocompatible and non-toxic on human HepG2 cells. As shown in Fig. 5f, the merged image indicates the ability of N,P-CDs to penetrate into the cell membrane without any additional surface passivating treatment. Therefore, these N,P-CDs are promising low cytotoxicity, fairly well cell permeability, stronger fluorescence staining agents for bioimaging applications, and can be used to multicolor cellular image in the optical window of biomatter.

Based on the outstanding properties of N,P-CDs, they can be explored as the fluorescent probing candidates for direct determination of some important biomacromolecules. Therefore, the fluorescence quenching effects of some common biomolecules and various chemical substances on N,P-CDs in water solution were observed. Different biomolecules including some important proteins, amino acids, nucleotides, and Hb and so on, each at a concentration of 5 μM, were added into 0.5 mg/mL N,P-CDs dispersion (Fig. 6 a and b). Various chemical substances with concentrations of 1 mM were also added into 0.5 mg/mL N,P-CDs dispersion (Fig. 6c). As shown in Fig. 6, almost no obvious reduction in the fluorescence intensity of N,P-CDs can be observed except for Hb. In addition, other heme proteins, such as cytochrome C (Cyt-C), protoporphyrin IX (Pro IX), and myoglobin, exhibit almost no influence on the fluorescence intensity of N,P-CDs. Numerous surface chemical groups of N,P-CDs maintain the tight binding action of N,P-CDs with Hb, and four iron-containing heme parts of Hb insure the strong π – π stacking action of Hb with N,P-CDs, which results in that N,P-CDs can be used as fluorescence probes for Hb with ultrahigh selectivity.

Our previous report indicates that Hb can quench the fluorescence of CDs through static quenching mode [22], so Hb may also quench the fluorescence of N,P-CDs by static quenching mechanism. As illustrated in Fig. 7 a, the average fluorescence lifetime of N,P-CDs is calculated to be 6.41 ns [22,33]. When Hb is present, the average fluorescence lifetime of N,P-CDs is still around 6.40 ns. Since the fluorescence lifetime of fluorophore remains constant in static quenching process but changes in dynamic quenching process [33], the fluorescence quenching mechanism of N,P-CDs-Hb system is ascribed to the static quenching mode. Such conclusion can be further confirmed by UV–vis absorption spectrometry. As shown in Fig. S12, the UV–vis absorption spectrum of N,P-CDs-Hb system and the sum absorption spectrum between N,P-CDs and Hb are not superposed within the experimental error, indicating that the ground state complex formation between N,P-CDs and Hb results in the static fluorescence quenching mechanism of N,P-CDs-Hb system.

The ground state complex formation between N,P-CDs and Hb can affect the secondary structure of Hb consequently [22], which can be confirmed through CD spectrometry. CD spectrometry is an effective strategy to directly investigate the structure variation of protein after their interactions with other molecules [32]. As shown in Fig. 7b, after the addition of N,P-CDs, the absorbance of two negative absorption peaks at 208 nm and 222 nm of Hb decreases significantly, indicating the variation of the secondary structure of HGB, especially the α-helix structure. The α-helical content of Hb can be calculated by the equations reported previously [32]. When the concentration of N,P-CDs increases from 0 to 3.0 mg/mL, the calculated α-helix structure of Hb decreases from 34.4% to 9.9%. The obvious decreasing tendency of the α-helical content of Hb is estimated at the present of N,P-CDs with higher concentration, indicating the disturbance of the hydrogen bonding networks of Hb. This result also suggests the reduction of the biological activity of Hb after its binding with N,P-CDs.

Based on the fluorescence property of N,P-CDs-Hb system, N,P-CDs can be utilized as the fluorescent probes for direct determination of Hb with high selectivity. In order to enhance the sensitivity of this approach, some experimental parameters were optimized: (a) Amount of N,P-CDs; (b) pH value of phosphate buffer; (c) Reaction time. As shown in Figs. S13, S14, and S15, the optimal experimental conditions are found to give best result: (a) Amount of N,P-CDs of 0.5 mg/mL; (b) pH value of 7.5; (c) Reaction time of 3 min. Under the optimal experimental conditions, fluorescence spectra of N,P-CDs with different concentration of Hb were recorded. As shown in Fig. 8 a, the fluorescence of N,P-CDs is quenched efficiently by Hb with the increasing concentration. The obvious blue emission of N,P-CDs becomes dark after the addition of 10 μM Hb (insert in Fig. 8a). The relationship between fluorescence intensity of N,P-CDs and concentration of Hb was further investigated. As shown in Fig. 8b, the variation of fluorescence intensity of N,P-CDs is sensitive to the concentration of Hb, which can be described by the well-known Stern-Volmer equation: I 0/I = 1 + K SV[Hb] (K SV is the Stern-Volmer quenching constant, and I 0 and I represent the fluorescence intensity of N,P-CDs in the absence and presence of Hb, respectively). A good linear relationship exists between the variation of fluorescence intensity of N,P-CDs and the concentration of Hb from 1 to 6000 nM with a good correlation coefficient of 0.9978 (insert in Fig. 8b). The relative standard deviation for 100 nM Hb was 0.15% (n = 5). Based on the three times standard deviation of 12 measurements of the blank signal of N,P-CDs, the limit of detection for Hb is calculated to be 0.29 nM that is comparable to the sensitive strategies recently reported (Table S3).

To confirm the feasibility, N,P-CDs fluorescent probes were utilized to detect Hb in human urine samples and human blood samples. As indicated in Table S4 and Table S5, the values of Hb found for five human urine samples and three human blood samples are highly identical with the spiked values. The recoveries are obtained in the range of 98.0% to 104.5%, and the relative standard deviation (n = 5) is less than 0.97%, which validates the reliability and the practicality of developed N,P-CDs based fluorescent probes for monitoring Hb in biological samples.

In summary, the synthesis of N,P-CDs with super fluorescent property and excellent biocompatibility through simple and rapid low-temperature heating approach was demonstrated. The structure of N,P-CDs was adequately characterized by HRTEM, FT-IR, XPS, and elemental analysis. The as-prepared N,P-CDs exhibit high aqueous solubility and excellent fluorescent stability even under extreme pH, ultrahigh ionic strengths, and longtime UV light irradiation. Due to dual-doping of nitrogen and phosphorus atoms, these N,P-CDs exhibit excellent fluorescent stability, favorable biocompatibility, and low cytotoxicity on human HepG2 cells, which are most important for biological and biomedical applications of N,P-CDs. These N,P-CDs emitting strong blue fluorescence were used as novel probes for highly sensitive detection of Hb with detection limit as low as 0.29 nM. The results for in vitro multicolor cellular imaging and monitoring Hb in biological samples successfully indicated that the super fluorescent property and excellent biocompatibility of N,P-CDs were promising as an efficient platform for desirable applications in analytical areas.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by National Natural Science Foundation of China (21763005, 21563006, 21403039), Natural Science Foundation of Guangxi Province (2015GXNSFAA139033, 2016GXNSFBA380118, 2017GXNSFDA198034, 2017GXNSFFA198005), Guangxi Scientific and Technological Development Projects (AD17195081), the high-level-innovation team (guijiaoren[2017]38) and outstanding scholar project of Guangxi Higher Education Institutes, BAGUI Scholar Program of Guangxi Province of China, and Project of Outstanding Young Teachers’ Training in Higher Education Institutions of Guangxi.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.snb.2018.03.056.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following is Supplementary data to this article:

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Quantum-sized carbon dots for bright and colorful photoluminescence
Heteroatom-doped carbon dots: synthesis, characterization, properties, photoluminescence mechanism and biological applications
Highly photoluminescent carbon dots for multicolor patterning sensors, and bioimaging
Hollow luminescent carbon dots for drug delivery
Inner filter with carbon quantum dots: a selective sensing platform for detection of hematin in human red cells
Preparation of yellow-green-emissive carbon dots and their application in constructing a fluorescent turn-on nanoprobe for imaging of selenol in living cells
Carbon nanodots: synthesis, properties and applications
A facile ultrasonic-assisted fabrication of nitrogen-doped carbon dots/BiOBr up-conversion nanocomposites for visible light photocatalytic enhancements
Microwave-assisted one-pot synthesis of highly luminescent N-doped carbon dots for cellular imaging and multi-ion probing
Sulfur-incorporated carbon quantum dots with a strong long-wavelength absorption band
Facile synthesis of P-doped carbon quantum dots with highly efficient photoluminescence
Dual functional N- and S-co-doped carbon dots as the sensor for temperature and Fe3+ ions
Phosphorus and nitrogen dual-doped hollow carbon dot as a nanocarrier for doxorubicin delivery and biological imaging
Carbonization conditions influence the emission characteristics and the stability against photobleaching of nitrogen doped carbon dots
One-pot and ultrafast synthesis of nitrogen and phosphorus co-doped carbon dots possessing bright dual wavelength fluorescence emission
Nitrogen and phosphorus co-doped graphene quantum dots: synthesis from adenosine triphosphate, optical properties, and cellular imaging
Nitrogen and phosphorus co-doped carbon nanodots as a novel fluorescent probe for highly sensitive detection of Fe3+ in human serum and living cells
Stereochemistry of cooperative effects in haemoglobin: haem–haem interaction and the problem of allostery
Facile synthesis of N-acetyl-L-cysteine capped CdHgSe quantum dots and selective determination of hemoglobin
Hemoglobin detection using carbon dots as a fluorescence probe
A carbon dots based fluorescent probe for selective and sensitive detection of hemoglobin
Economical, green synthesis of fluorescent carbon nanoparticles and their use as probes for sensitive and selective detection of Mercury (II) ions
Microwave assisted one-pot synthesis of grapheme quantum dots as highly sensitive fluorescent probes for detection of iron ions and pH value
One-pot synthesis of boron and nitrogen co-doped carbon dots as the fluorescence probe for dopamine based on the redox reaction between Cr(VI) and dopamine
Nitrogen-doped carbon-rich, highly photoluminescent carbon dots from ammonium citrate
Highly selective electrochemical strategy for monitoring of cerebral Cu2+ based on a carbon dot-TPEA hybridized surface
Nitrogen-doped carbon dots derived from polyvinyl pyrrolidone and their multicolor cell imaging
Low temperature synthesis of highly stable phosphate functionalized two color carbon nanodots and their application in cell imaging
pH-sensitive carbon dots for the visualization of regulation of intracellular pH inside living pathogenic fungal cells
Sulfur and nitrogen binary doped carbon dots derived from ammonium thiocyanate for selective probing doxycycline in living cells and multicolor cell imaging
Synchrotron radiation circular dichroism spectroscopy of proteins and applications in structural and functional genomics
Fluorescence lifetime imaging microscopy for the detection of intracellular pH with quantum dot nanosensors

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Shan Huang received her PhD degree in Analytical Chemistry from Wuhan University in 2009. After a two-year postdoctoral (2009–2011) stay at the Institut Armand-Frappier, Institut National de la Recherche Scientifique (INRS) and INRS-Énergie, Matériaux et Télécommunications, Université du Québec, Canada, she returned to China as an associate professor at Guangxi Teachers Education University. Her current research interests are the synthesis of functional nanomaterials and application in biochemical sensors.
Erli Yang is pursuing his master degree in Analytical Chemistry at Guangxi Teachers Education University. He works on the development of novel carbon-based nanomaterials for fluorescent sensors.

Yi Liu is a Professor of Chemistry and Chemical Biology at College of Chemistry and Molecular Sciences, Wuhan University, PR China. He obtained BS in 1992 and PhD in 1997 from Wuhan University, China National Funds for Distinguished Young Scientists in 2012. His research interests include development of thermal analytical and spectroscopic methods for assaying biological complex system, targeted pharmacy and biological effect of nanoparticles. So far, he has published over 200 papers and those papers have been cited for about 3000 times.
Jiandong Yao is pursuing his master degree in Analytical Chemistry at Guangxi Teachers Education University. He works on the development of novel nanomaterials for fluorescent sensors.

Wei Su received his PhD degree in Inorganic Chemistry from Shandong University in 2007. Now, he is a professor at Guangxi Teachers Education University. His current research interests are the synthesis of functional ruthenium anticancer drugs and their interactions with DNA.

Qi Xiao received his PhD degree in Physical Chemistry from Wuhan University in 2010. After a one-year postdoctoral (2010–2011) stay at University of Massachusetts, USA, he returned to China as a professor at Guangxi Teachers Education University. His current research interests are the synthesis of functional graphene nanomaterials and their application in biological sensors.
